今年1月，瑞典企业家约阿基姆·胡尔廷(Joakim Hultin)与人共同创立了SideHide，这是一款旨在简化酒店预订的新数字应用程序。几周后，欧洲报告了一些首批确诊的-19病例。几乎立刻，胡尔丁告诉我，“需求停止了。”在大流行之前，SideHide正在与一家总部位于伦敦的公司Onfido合作，该公司使用人工智能和面部识别来验证身份。Hultin了解到，Onfido已经为用户创造了一种方法，可以将血清学测试上传到私人服务器，并使用面部生物识别数据来解锁数据并显示结果。他要求公司将这种能力构建到他的应用程序中。今年6月，SideHide将在迈阿密的几家酒店再次推出，增加了所谓的“豁免护照”。接受过-19抗体检测并被证明携带-19抗体的顾客将把这些信息嵌入二维码，由酒店工作人员在抵达时进行扫描。迈阿密的启动是一次考验，一次概念的证明，如果成功，Hultin希望它将有助于重振旅游业。(达美航空公司(Delta Air Lines)和希思罗机场(Heathrow Airport)的官员已经表达了对豁免护照的兴趣。)。胡尔丁说，这样做的目的是让酒店员工知道“客人是安全的”。
今年4月，Onfido筹集了1亿美元的风险投资。在宣布这笔交易的新闻稿中，Onfido的律师指出，大流行为公司提供了“一系列新的使用案例”的机会，范围从“虚拟投票和护照/签证申请，到进行联系人追踪的安全方法，以在不损害用户隐私的情况下跟踪病毒的传播”。最近，Onfido向英国议会科学技术委员会的成员提交了一份提案，当时他们正在考虑取消居家命令的方法，包括开发豁免护照。国会议员们正在追随智利政府的脚步，智利政府正在发布文件，允许任何从-19岁康复的人在隔离期间重返工作岗位，自由活动。(Onfido没有参与该项目。)。智利医学界成员和世界各地的传染病专家表示，现在就知道感染病毒的人是否对再次感染具有免疫力或不能传播疾病还为时过早。之后，政府开始将这些文件称为“放行证明”，而不是免疫护照。在此之后，智利政府开始将这些文件称为“释放证书”，而不是免疫护照。这些专家表示，现在就知道感染病毒的人是否对再次感染具有免疫力还为时过早。无论哪种方式，它们都会带来同样的好处。Onfido的首席执行官侯赛因·卡赛告诉我，“作为一个社会，对于那些感染了病毒并康复的人来说，发出这一信号是可以接受的吗？这是一个很好的问题，但不是我们应该回答的问题。“。
其他公司也在推销类似的产品。-Pass是一种豁免护照，使用VCode-一种专有的密码图像，类似于二维码-可以在100米远的距离进行扫描。它将显示一个人的抗体测试是否呈阳性，如果一个人的病毒测试为阴性，它将闪烁绿色，如果测试显示他们有病毒或没有抗体，或者他们的测试结果是否过期，它将显示红色。(黄灯显示何时需要重新测试。)。这款应用程序可以对入口点进行地理围栏，禁止闯红灯的游客进入建筑物、体育场或学校。PASS首席执行官亚当·帕尔默(Adam Palmer)说，“我相信，所有政府都会朝着全球健康护照迈进，这将和持有驾照或护照一样普遍，因为这不会是我们最后一次大流行。”同样位于英国的该公司最近的一份新闻稿称，通行证正在运往“超过15个国家的私营部门和政府”，包括意大利、法国、印度和美国。-PASS不会具体说明这些国家的哪些公司和政府购买了该产品。但VST企业的首席执行官、VCode的创始人路易斯-詹姆斯·戴维斯(Louis-James Davis)确实提到，该公司的V-Health Passport将于今年夏天在英国的体育场馆推出。V-Health Passport也记录了用户的19岁身份。在接近大门之前，必须扫描护照才能在网上购买门票，然后在体育场再次扫描。
豁免护照的部署引起了一些后勤和道德方面的关切。在美国，检测可能成为获得通行证的障碍：诊断检测一直受到限制，测量-19种抗体的血清学测试仍在开发中。即使检测无处不在，没有抗体的人也会被日常生活活动拒之门外，造成抗体有无的分裂局面。“现实情况是，通过[这种]认证进入社会可能会反映出系统中存在的系统偏见、腐败和歧视，”In的助理教授亚历山德拉·费兰(Alexandra Phelan)说。
一群科学家在国防部研究部门的支持下工作，正在寻求一条不同的道路。科学家们正在开发一种测试病毒引起的基因表达的特定宿主签名的方法-即所谓的表观遗传学-以在出现症状之前很久就检测到-19的存在。生物技术办公室的项目经理埃里克·范·吉森(Eric Van Gieson)正在监督这项工作，他告诉我，我们的目标是创造一种易于使用、流行程度与链球菌检测或流感检测一样普遍的检测方法，你可以在医生办公室进行这种检测。范吉森说：“我们希望尽早确认更多人的身份。”“我们还希望确保我们不会依赖病毒脱落进行诊断。”这一点至关重要，因为一旦有人感染了病毒，除非他们被隔离，否则他们就会传播病毒。一旦有了有效的治疗方法，这种检测将特别有用，但即使在没有有效治疗的情况下，它也有能力通过防止无症状的人成为疾病的媒介来减缓病毒的传播。而且，由于表观遗传学还利用人体的先天免疫记忆来揭示一个人何时接触过病原体，当与强大的接触者追踪相结合时，这项测试可以防止集群爆发。如果-19抗体被证明没有保护性，或者如果它们提供的保护是有限的，或者如果社会拒绝免疫护照计划，频繁的表观遗传测试可能是绕过大规模封锁的一种方式。
范·吉森不会对表观遗传测试的生产设定时间表，因为仍有研究需要审查，监管程序需要谈判。他确实说过，参与这项努力的科学家们，其中一些人已经感染了-19病毒，正在不间断地工作。与此同时，政府和企业在要求结束封锁的财政和社会压力越来越大的情况下，可能会发现等待科学研究在政治和经济上都很麻烦。但是，Van Gieson告诉我，“我们知道有一种治疗方法，我们知道有一种诊断工具。我们知道市面上有一种疫苗。所以让科学来做这件事，对吗？我的意思是，归根结底，真相将取决于科学，而不是人们说了什么或人们是如何反应的，比如恐慌等等。你只需要做彻底、严谨的科学工作，并且只相信你的数据。别再相信其他的了。“。

note_en:
In January, a Swedish entrepreneur named Joakim Hultin co-founded Sidehide, a new digital app intended to streamline hotel reservations. Weeks later, some of the first confirmed cases of  -19 were reported in Europe. Almost instantly, Hultin told me, “demand stopped.” Before  the pandemic, Sidehide was working with a London-based company called Onfido, which uses artificial intelligence and facial recognition to verify identities. Hultin learned that Onfido had created a way for users to upload a serology test to a private server and use facial biometric data to unlock the data and display the results. He asked the company to build that capacity into his app. In June, Sidehide will launch again, in a few Miami hotels, having added what is being called an “immunity passport.” Patrons who have been tested for  -19 antibodies and are shown to have them will have that information embedded in a QR code, to be scanned by hotel staff upon arrival. The Miami launch is a test, a proof of concept, and, if it works, Hultin is hopeful that it will help revive the travel industry. (Officials from Delta Air Lines and Heathrow Airport have expressed interest in immunity passports.) The idea, Hultin said, is to let the hotel staff know “the guest is safe.”
  In April, Onfido raised a hundred million dollars in venture capital. In a press release announcing the deal, Onfido’s attorneys noted that the pandemic presented the company with the opportunity “to work on a new set of use cases,” which range from “virtual voting and passport/visa applications through to secure ways of carrying out contact tracing to track the spread of a virus without compromising user privacy.” Recently, Onfido submitted a proposal to members of the U.K. Parliament’s science and technology committee, as they consider ways to lift stay-at-home orders, including the development of immunity passports. The M.P.s are following the lead of the Chilean government, which is issuing documents that allow anyone who has recovered from  -19 to go back to work and move around freely during quarantine. (Onfido has not worked on that program.) After  pushback from members of the Chilean medical community—and from infectious-disease specialists around the world, who say that it’s too early to know if people who have had the virus are immune to reinfection or incapable of spreading the disease—the government  started calling the documents “release certificates” rather than immunity passports. Either way, they confer the same benefits. Onfido’s C.E.O., Husayn Kassai, told me, “As a society, is it acceptable for people who have had the virus and recovered to signal that or not? It’s a good question, but not one for us to answer.”
   Other companies are pitching similar products.  -Pass is an immunity passport that uses VCode—a proprietary cryptographic image, similar to a QR code—that can be scanned at a distance of a hundred metres. It will reveal if a person has tested positive for antibodies, flash green if a person has tested negative for the virus, and red if tests show that they have the virus or don’t have antibodies, or if their test result has expired. (A yellow light shows when it’s time to be retested.) The app can “geo-fence” a point of entry, prohibiting visitors with a red light from entering a building or stadium or school. “I believe that all governments will move toward a global health passport, which will be as common as carrying a driver’s license or a passport, because this is not going to be the last pandemic we have,” Adam Palmer, the C.O.O. of  -Pass, said. A recent press release from the company, which is also based in the U.K., states that the passes were in the process of being shipped “to both the private sector and governments in over fifteen countries,” including Italy, France, India, and the United States.  -Pass would not say, specifically, which companies and governments in these countries had purchased the product. But Louis-James Davis, the C.E.O. of VST Enterprises and the creator of VCode, did mention that the company’s V-Health Passport, which also documents users’  -19 status, would be rolled out at sports venues in the U.K. this summer. The passport will have to be scanned in order to buy a ticket online, and then again at the stadium, before approaching the gate.
 The deployment of immunity passports raises a number of logistical and ethical concerns. In the United States, testing could become a barrier to getting a pass: diagnostic testing has  been limited, and serology tests, which measure  -19 antibodies, are still being developed. Even if testing were universally available, people without antibodies will be shut out from the activities of everyday life, creating a divided landscape of antibody haves and have-nots. “The reality is that access to society with [this kind of] certification will likely reflect existing systemic biases, corruption, and discrimination in the system,” Alexandra Phelan, an assistant professor in infectious diseases and law at Georgetown University, told me. And, if testing positive for  -19 antibodies becomes a prerequisite for being able to work, there may be a perverse incentive for people to put themselves in the path of the virus. “This would not be like bringing your kids to chicken-pox parties,” Phelan said. “Individuals who have economic pressures to get back to work may already be in socioeconomically vulnerable positions, and we know that these are the people who are disproportionately more likely to get more severely ill.”
 Rob Davidson, an emergency-room physician in western Michigan and the executive director of the Committee to Protect Medicare, an advocacy group, worries that immunity passports based on tests with high rates of false positives will be especially pernicious. “If we have tests with a five-per-cent false-positive rate, and you are forced to take a test that is not very good, and you test positive, you will have a false sense of security,” he told me. “The government and the employers probably know the shortcomings of the test, but a lot of the workers probably don’t.” In those cases, immunity passports may increase the risk of infection to both their holders and to the community at large. (Both  -Pass and Sidehide say that they are partnering with local, well-regarded testing facilities.)
  Privacy, too, may be at risk, even with immunity passports that do nothing more than display a user’s test results. “We are in a less-than-ideal situation with the coronavirus,” Onfido’s Kassai told me. “This is one of the ways people can signal they are safe without divulging personal information.” But immunity status  is personal information. If a person’s immunity status were linked to an insurance policy, or attached to an employee’s human-resources file, it could cause rates to rise, or promotions to be denied. Already, officials at the U.S. Defense Department have proposed  banning anyone who has had  -19 from enlisting in the military. And a recent story in the  Times  inventories a long list of people who have been shunned as a consequence of others knowing their disease status: “The veterinarian who refused to treat a recovered woman’s dog. The laundromat worker who jumped at seeing an elected official whose illness had been reported on the local news. The gardener who would not trim the hedges outside a recovered man’s home. The neighbor who dropped off soup, and said not to bother returning the Tupperware it was in. And the sick teenager whose solace during his long illness was the thought of fishing with friends, only to have them ghost him when he recovered.”
  There remains another obstacle to using  -19 antibodies as a delimiting metric: science. The biology of this novel coronavirus is so new that no one knows whether  -19 antibodies actually confer immunity—and, if they do, for how long. In April, the World Health Organization  issued a scientific brief that concluded that “there is not enough evidence about the effectiveness of antibody-mediated immunity to guarantee the accuracy of an ‘immunity passport’ or ‘risk-free certificate.’ ” Even the most precise serological test, from the Swiss pharmaceutical company Roche, which is reported to be a hundred-per-cent accurate at detecting the coronavirus antibodies, can only determine the presence of  -19 antibodies—it can’t say what they mean. “We hope antibodies will be protective, but we don't know for sure yet.” Tom Frieden, was the director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention during the Obama Administration, told me. “It now appears likely that at least some infections will provide at least some degree of immunity. Which infections, how much immunity, and for how long are crucially important but still unanswered questions.”
 Frieden added that, if these questions could be resolved—and if  a vaccine were still absent—he could imagine a role for immunity passports in certain circumstances. “For example, you might want to make sure that only people who—if there is documented immunity—work in nursing homes to the greatest extent possible with non-immune residents, because that’s a population of enormous concern if immunity is confirmed,” he said. “I think it’s too soon to say that these will be useful, but it’s not too soon to begin thinking about how they might be used if antibodies are indeed protective.”
 A group of scientists, working under the auspices of  , the research arm of the Defense Department, is pursuing a different path. The scientists are developing a method of testing for certain host signatures of gene expression caused by the virus—what is known as epigenetics—to detect the presence of  -19 well before there are symptoms. Eric Van Gieson, a program manager at the Biological Technologies Office, who is overseeing this effort, told me that the goal is to create a test that is as easy to use and as prevalent as a strep test or a test for flu that you might take at the doctor’s office. “We want to identify as many people as early as possible,” Van Gieson said. “We also want to make sure that we’re not depending on viral shedding for diagnosis.” This is crucial, because, once someone is shedding the virus, they will be spreading it unless they are isolated. This test will be especially useful once there are effective treatments, but even in their absence it has the capacity to slow the spread of the virus by preventing asymptomatic people from becoming vectors of disease. And, because epigenetics also exploits the body’s innate immune memory to reveal when a person has been exposed to a pathogen, this test could prevent cluster outbreaks when paired with robust contact tracing. If  -19 antibodies do not turn out to be protective, or if the protection they offer is limited, or if societies reject immunity-passport schemes, frequent epigenetic tests may be a way to bypass large-scale lockdowns.
 Van Gieson would not put a time line on the production of an epigenetic test, because there is still research to be reviewed and regulatory procedures to negotiate. He did say that scientists involved in the effort, some of whom have been sick with  -19, are working nonstop. In the meantime, governments and businesses, under increasing financial and social pressures to end lockdowns, may find it politically and economically troublesome to wait for the science. But, Van Gieson told me, “We know there’s a cure out there and we know there’s a diagnostic tool out there. We know there’s a vaccine out there. So let the science do the work, right? I mean, at the end of the day, the truth is going to come down to the science of it, not what people say or how people react—the panic, all that stuff. You just have to do thorough, rigorous scientific work and only believe your data. Don’t believe anything else.”

    The coronavirus is  likely to spread for more than a year before a vaccine is widely available.
 How to practice  social distancing, from responding to a sick housemate to the pros and cons of  ordering food.
 The long crusade of  Dr. Anthony Fauci, the infectious-disease expert pinned between Donald Trump and the American people.
title: 豁免护照与冠状病毒身份授予的危害
title_en: Immunity Passports and the Perils of Conferring Coronavirus Status

跳转到导航跳转搜索这是一个常见误解列表。每个条目都被格式化为更正；错误概念本身是隐含的，而不是陈述的。这些条目都很简洁，但更多细节可以在主要主题文章中找到。
灼伤不会密封湿气。烧焦的肉与没有烧焦的等量肉相比，实际上会使它失去水分。一般来说，烤肉的价值在于它通过美拉德反应产生一种味道浓郁的棕色外壳。[3][4]。
没有证据支持谷氨酸钠(MSG)会引发偏头痛加重或其他所谓的中餐馆综合症症状。虽然已经有关于味精敏感人群子集的报道，但这还没有在安慰剂对照试验中得到证实。[5][6]。
Twinkies的保质期约为45天[7](按最初的配方为25天)--远远短于常见的(有点开玩笑的)神话，即Twinkies可以食用几十年或更长时间。[8]它们通常只在商店货架上保存7到10天。[9]。
尽管幸运饼干在美国与中国菜肴联系在一起，但它是在日本发明的，并由日本人引入美国。[1]这种饼干在中国极为罕见，在那里它们被视为美国美食的象征。[2]。
有毒糖果和水果的故事已被彻底揭穿。还没有陌生人以这种方式杀害或永久伤害儿童的案例被证明，也没有陌生人用有毒的糖果或苹果伤害儿童的报道。对有毒糖果的焦虑可能源于媒体对罗纳德·克拉克·布莱恩(Ronald Clark O&#39；Bryan)的报道。布莱恩是一位父亲，在万圣节给自己的儿子吃了含氰化物的糖果。[10]。
除了一些易腐烂的食物外，大多数食物在保质期过后很长一段时间都是可以食用的。[11][12]。
种子不是辣椒的辛辣部分。事实上，种子含有少量的辣椒素，辣椒素是哺乳动物产生热感的成分。辣椒素的最高浓度位于种子附着的胎盘组织中。[13]
微波炉不是通过在食物中水分子的特殊共振下工作来加热食物的。微波炉的工作原理是电介质加热，而不是水的共振频率，因此微波炉可以在许多频率下工作。水分子暴露在强非共振微波中的强电磁场中产生热量。孤立水分子的22 GHz共振频率的波长太短，无法将普通食品穿透到有用的深度。选择2.45千兆赫的典型烤箱频率，部分是因为它能够穿透合理大小的食物物体，部分是为了避免在微波炉商业化时对使用中的通信频率造成干扰。[14]。
微波炉不会从里到外烹调食物。2.45 GHz微波只能穿透大约1厘米(0.39英寸)到大多数食物中。较厚食物的内部主要由外部传导的热量加热。[15][16]。
微波炉不会致癌，因为微波辐射是非电离的，因此不存在与X射线等电离辐射相关的癌症风险。没有关于与微波相关的癌症风险的研究发现微波辐射有任何致癌作用，即使暴露水平远远高于人类从泄漏的烤箱中可能遇到的水平。[17][18]。
很少需要等待24小时才能提交失踪人员报告。在有暴力或异常缺席的证据的情况下，美国的执法机构经常强调迅速开始调查的重要性。[19]英国政府网站上用大号字体写着，你不必等24小时就能联系警察。
旧金山市长乔治·莫斯科内和主管哈维·米尔克的谋杀案并未被宣称是Twinkies的罪魁祸首。在对丹·怀特的审判中，辩方成功地辩称，由于严重的抑郁症，怀特的能力减弱了。虽然吃夹心饼干被认为是抑郁症的证据，但从来没有人声称它是谋杀的原因。尽管如此，人们经常说怀特的律师辩称是Twinkies让他犯下了谋杀案。[21]。
自20世纪80年代以来，美国武装部队普遍禁止将入伍作为一种推迟判决的形式(即罪犯避免坐牢的一种选择)。美国海军的协议不鼓励这种做法，而其他四个分支机构有具体的规定反对这种做法。[22][23]。
美国的法定货币法律没有规定私人企业、个人或组织必须接受现金支付。[24][25][26]。
在美国[27]或英国，将自己制作的文件邮寄给自己并不会带来额外的版权保护。[28][28]
美国不要求警察在从事诱捕或其他卧底工作时必须表明自己是警察，警察在从事这类工作时可能会撒谎。[29]相反，声称诱捕作为辩护的重点是被告是否受到来自执法部门的过度压力(如威胁)或欺骗的诱使，犯下了他们本来不会犯下的罪行。[30][30]。
1993年至2017年间，美国的暴力犯罪有所下降。暴力犯罪率在此期间下降了49%，尽管大多数美国人认为枪支犯罪的数量有所上升。[32][font=宋体]。
美国宪法第一修正案一般禁止政府限制宗教、言论、新闻、集会或请愿自由，[33]而不是个人或企业施加的限制[34]，除非他们代表政府行事。[35]其他法律可能会限制私营企业和个人限制他人言论的能力。[36]。
黑手党和其他犯罪组织都没有使用胶鞋溺死受害者。这种方法只在单个情况下用于淹没(已经)身体。[37]。
雪绒花不是奥地利国歌，而是为音乐剧“音乐之声”创作的原创作品。[38]奥地利国歌是#34；Land der Berge，Land am Strome&34；(&#34；Land of the Mountain，Land on the River)。[39]。
菲尔·柯林斯没有演唱他1981年的热门歌曲“今夜在空中”，他说的是目睹有人溺水，然后与观众中的肇事者对峙。[40]。
“闪烁，闪烁，小星”，“字母歌”和“哔，哔，黑羊”中使用的旋律不是沃尔夫冈·阿马迪乌斯·莫扎特在5岁时创作的；在莫扎特25岁或26岁创作这首曲子的一系列变奏曲之前，它已经是一种流行的法国民谣曲调，已经存在了几十年。(注：沃尔夫冈·阿马迪乌斯·莫扎特(Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart)在他25岁或26岁的时候创作了一系列变奏曲，在此之前，它已经是一种流行的法国民谣曲调了几十年。[41]。
沃尔夫冈·阿马迪乌斯·莫扎特不是奥地利人。在莫扎特的一生中，萨尔茨堡不是奥地利大公国的一部分，而是神圣罗马帝国内一个被称为萨尔茨堡大主教亲王的主权国家。1805年，在莫扎特去世14年后，萨尔茨堡并入奥地利帝国。此外，在作曲家的时代，奥地利人这个词指的是前面提到的奥地利大公国、奥地利圈或哈布斯堡君主制，这两个都不包括萨尔茨堡，它是巴伐利亚圈的一部分。[42][43][43]
自从沃尔夫冈·阿马迪乌斯·莫扎特去世以来，一直有传言说，这位作曲家是被他的同事安东尼奥·萨列里毒死的。这已经被证明是错误的，因为莫扎特显示的疾病迹象并不表明中毒。[44]这一误解得以延续的一个重要因素是1984年著名的电影《阿马迪乌斯》，在这部电影中，萨列里声称他杀死了莫扎特。
在YouTube上一个非常流行的帖子中，保罗·德·塞内维尔(Paul De Senneville)的钢琴独奏曲目被误认为是弗雷德里克·肖邦(Frédéric Chopin)的作品，被错误地称为“春天华尔兹”，尽管它不是华尔兹。[46][中英文对照]。
克里斯蒂安·佩佐德(Christian Petzold)的G大调小步舞曲通常被认为是约翰·塞巴斯蒂安·巴赫(Johann Sebastian Bach)的作品，尽管这首曲子在20世纪70年代被认定为佩佐德的大键琴组曲中的乐章。这种误解源于安娜·马格达莱娜·巴赫的笔记本，这是一本由不同作曲家(主要是巴赫)创作的乐谱，小步舞曲就是在这本书中找到的。[47][48][49][50]被怀疑为巴赫作品的作曲编目为BWV An.，简称为Bach-Werke-Verzeichnis Anang；巴赫作品目录附件，小步舞步分配给BWV Anh。114.。
据了解，这位历史上的佛像并不肥胖。这位胖乎乎的佛像是10世纪的中国民间英雄，名叫布代。在中国佛教文化中，布代被尊崇为弥勒菩萨的化身，弥勒菩萨在历史上的释迦牟尼菩萨(Siddhārtha Gautama)的教导被遗忘后，将成为佛陀以恢复佛教。[51][font=宋体]。
“创世纪”中提到的禁果从未被认定为苹果[52]，这是西方艺术中广泛描绘的一个误解。希伯来语原文只提到树木和水果。早期的拉丁语翻译使用“马里”这个词，这个词的意思可以是邪恶的，也可以是苹果的，这取决于A是短的还是长的，尽管元音长度的差异在当时的拉丁语中已经消失了。在早期日耳曼语言中，苹果一词及其同源词通常只表示水果。从12世纪开始，德国和法国的艺术家通常把这种水果描绘成苹果，而约翰·弥尔顿1644年出版的“Areopagitica”则明确提到这种水果是苹果。[53]犹太学者认为，这种水果可能是葡萄、无花果、杏子或茶树。[54][54]。
虽然他们是被利未记禁止的，但纹身并不意味着一个人不能像人们通常认为的那样被埋葬在犹太人的墓地里，就像违反任何其他禁令并不能阻止犹太人最终被埋葬在犹太人的墓地一样。[55][font=宋体]。
“圣经”没有说恰好有三位麦琪来看望婴儿耶稣，也没有说他们是国王，也没有骑骆驼，也没有说他们的名字是卡斯帕、梅尔基奥和巴尔萨扎。这三个麦琪是隐含的，因为描述了三个礼物，但我们只知道它们是复数(至少2个麦琪)，但也可能有更多，可能包括一个陪同他们旅行的随行人员。自公元3世纪以来，对耶稣诞生的艺术描绘几乎总是描绘了三个麦琪。[56]圣经只规定从出生到探访的时间间隔上限为2年(马太福音2：16)，艺术描绘和传统日期12月25日和1月6日的接近，助长了流行的假设，即探望发生在与出生相同的季节，但后来的传统有所不同，探望最多发生在两年后。与国王的联系来自于将这次访问与以赛亚书中的预言联系在一起的努力。[57][font=宋体]。
抹大拉的马利亚在遇见耶稣之前是妓女的想法，在圣经或任何其他最早的基督教著作中都找不到。这种误解很可能是由于抹大拉的马利亚、伯大尼的马利亚(在约翰福音11：1-12抹耶稣的脚)和路加福音7：36-50中抹耶稣脚的无名罪妇之间的融合而产生的。[58][58]
使徒保罗没有把他的名字从扫罗改过来。他出生为犹太人，从父亲那里继承了罗马国籍，因此从出生起就有一个希伯来语和一个拉丁语名字。路加福音指出使徒行传13：9：&34；……扫罗的名字是共存的，他也被称为保罗……。[59][60][60]。
术语“无瑕疵受孕”并不是指耶稣的处女出生，也不是指他的母亲玛利亚是处女出生的信仰。“无瑕疵受孕”这个词并不是指耶稣的处女出生，也不是指他的母亲玛利亚是处女出生的信仰。相反，它表明了罗马天主教的信仰，即玛丽从她自己受孕的那一刻起就没有处于原罪状态。[61][61]。
罗马天主教的教义并不是说教皇是无罪的，也不是说教皇总是不会犯错。[62]自1870年以来的天主教教义确实表明，教皇颁布的神圣启示中所包含的教条教义(故意地，在某些非常具体的情况下；通常称为前大教堂)是没有错误的，尽管官方援引教皇的正确性的情况很少。虽然大多数神学家说，封圣符合条件，[63]除此之外，大多数最近的教皇在没有一次祈祷无误的情况下完成了他们的统治。否则，即使是以官方身份讲话，教条也不能说他没有错误。
圣彼得大教堂不是罗马天主教的母教堂，也不是教皇的正式所在地。[64]这些等同的区别属于圣约翰·拉特兰大教堂，它位于梵蒂冈城外的罗马，但梵蒂冈对其拥有治外法权。[64]这也意味着圣彼得教堂不是字面意义上的大教堂。[64]然而，圣彼得教堂被用作许多教皇活动的主要教堂。[64]。
耶稣基督后期圣徒教会(LDS教会)的成员不再实行一夫多妻制。[65][66][67]目前，LDS教会将在该组织内实行一夫多妻制的任何成员逐出教会。[69]然而，一些摩门教原教旨主义教派仍然在他们的团体内实行一夫多妻制。[70]有关此主题的更多详细信息，请参阅摩门教和一夫多妻制。
布卡(也拼写为burka或burkha)是一种起源于中亚的女性穆斯林服装，它覆盖着女性的整个身体、头部和面部，包括她的眼睛，并有一个网窗或格栅让女性看到。这个词经常被错误地用来指女性穿的其他穆斯林服装，比如niqab(露出眼睛)和Hijab(露出整个脸，但隐藏了身体的其他部分，包括头发)。[72]虽然罩袍通常是浅蓝色或白色的，但面纱通常是黑色的，而头巾是一种可以是任何颜色的面纱。
Fat wā是伊斯兰学者根据伊斯兰法律发表的不具约束力的法律意见；因此，不同作者的Fatāwā持不同意见是司空见惯的。普遍的误解，认为这个词意味着死刑，可能源于伊朗的阿亚图拉·鲁霍拉·霍梅尼(Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini)1989年发布的关于作家萨尔曼·拉什迪(Salman Rushdie)的法特·拉什迪(Fateā)，他说，萨勒曼·拉什迪因亵渎神明而被判处死刑。这一事件导致胖子āwā在西方获得了广泛的媒体关注。[75][75]。
圣战这个词并不总是意味着圣战，从字面上看，这个词在阿拉伯语中的意思是斗争。虽然有“圣战”或“剑下圣战”这样的说法，但许多现代伊斯兰学者通常说，它暗示着一种精神上的努力或斗争。[77]学者Louay Safi断言，在9/11事件之后，穆斯林社会和西方社会都普遍存在着对伊斯兰战争与和平本质的误解和误解。[79][font=宋体]
古兰经并没有承诺烈士在天堂有72个处女。它确实提到了同伴，胡里，在天堂里的所有人-无论是不是烈士-都有，但没有具体的数字。这72位处女的来源是伊玛目提尔米迪在苏南提尔米迪的圣训。[80]哈迪斯是其他人所报道的先知穆罕默德的言论和行为，因此它们不是《古兰经》本身的一部分。穆斯林并不一定要相信所有的圣训，这尤其适用于那些来源薄弱的圣训，比如这一次。[82]此外，这一圣训的正确翻译是一个有争议的问题。[80]然而，在同一批逊尼派圣徒中，以下内容被认为是强有力的(哈桑·萨希)：&#34；真主对烈士有六件事。他第一次流血就被赦免了(他遭受了痛苦)，他在天堂的位置得到了展示，他在坟墓里得到了保护，免受了最大的恐怖，尊严的王冠戴在了他的头上--它的宝石比世界和里面的东西都要好--他在“天堂之歌”中娶了72个妻子，他可能会为他的70个近亲说情。&#34；[83]。
艾布纳·道布尔戴没有发明棒球，也不是起源于纽约的库珀斯敦。据信，它是从板球和圆球等其他蝙蝠和球运动演变而来的，最早是在纽约市采用现代形式的。[84][85][85]。
武术中的黑带并不一定代表专家的水平或熟练程度。它在19世纪80年代被引入柔道，以表明这项运动的所有基本技术的能力。第一段(第一黑带级别)以上的晋升在不同的武术中是不同的。在柔道和衍生武术(如巴西柔道)中，级别较高的大师会被授予交替的红色和白色嵌板，最高的大师会佩戴坚实的红色腰带。[86]其他艺术，如跆拳道，则使用带有许多金条的黑色腰带来表示持有者的丹等级。
在英格兰足球中使用三角形角旗并不是那些过去赢得足总杯的球队的特权，尽管人们普遍认为情况相反[87]，这激发了电影“双胞胎城”中的一个场景。足协的规则在这个问题上是沉默的，通常情况下，决定使用什么形状的旗帜是由个别俱乐部的场地管理员决定的。[88][font=宋体]。
印度并没有因为他们的球队赤脚踢球而退出1950年国际足联世界杯，这违反了国际足联的规定。[89]实际上，印度退出是因为该国的管理机构，全印度足球联合会(AIFF)对该队的参赛准备不足，并给出了各种退出的原因，包括缺乏资金和优先举办奥运会。[90]然而，这个神话经常作为事实在印度和国外重新出现(特别是在世界杯期间)。[91]AIFF本身可能是这个神话的来源。[90][90]。
说英语的人使用的不规范、俚语或口语化的术语有时被认为不是真正的单词，尽管在许多词典中都有出现。英语中的所有单词都是在一段时间内被普遍使用而被接受的；因此，目前有许多白话单词不被接受为标准语言的一部分，或者在正式的演讲或写作中被认为是不恰当的，但认为它们不知何故不是单词的想法是一种误解。[93]有时被称为不是单词的单词的例子包括：#34；无理取闹，[95][96]&#34；Conversate&#34；，&#34；Funnest&34；，[97]&#34；Mentee&#34；，&#34；Imactful&#34；和&#34；Thusly&#34；，[98]，所有这些词都作为英语单词出现在众多词典中。[99][99]。
这个词不是起源于基督化的盎格鲁-撒克逊英格兰(公元7世纪)，它不是在国王同意下通奸的首字母缩写；也不是作为非法肉体知识的首字母缩写，也不是作为张贴在股票通奸者上方的标志，也不是作为对英国人成员的刑事指控。“他妈的”这个词并不是源于基督教盎格鲁-撒克逊英格兰(公元7世纪)，也不是作为国王同意下的通奸的首字母缩写；也不是作为非法肉体知识的首字母缩写，也不是作为张贴在股票通奸者上方的标志，也不是作为对英国人成员的刑事指控。

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  This is a  list of common misconceptions. Each entry is formatted as a correction; the misconceptions themselves are implied rather than stated. These entries are meant to be concise, but more detail can be found in the main subject articles.
     Searing does not seal in moisture.  Searing meat actually causes it to lose moisture in comparison to an equivalent piece of meat cooked without searing. Generally, the value in searing meat is that it produces a brown crust with a rich flavor via the  Maillard reaction.  [3]  [4]
 There is no evidence supporting  monosodium glutamate (MSG) as triggering  migraine exacerbation or other symptoms of so-called  Chinese restaurant syndrome. Although there have been reports of an MSG-sensitive subset of the population, this has not been demonstrated in  placebo-controlled trials.  [5]  [6]
 Twinkies have a  shelf life of approximately 45 days  [7] (25 in their original formulation)—far shorter than the common (and somewhat jocular) myth that Twinkies are edible for decades or longer.  [8] They generally remain on a store shelf for only 7 to 10 days.  [9]
 Fortune cookies, despite being associated with  Chinese cuisine in the United States, were invented in Japan and introduced to the US by the Japanese.  [1] The cookies are extremely rare in China, where they are seen as symbols of American cuisine.  [2]
 Poisoned candy and fruit stories have been "thoroughly debunked". No cases of strangers killing or permanently injuring children this way have ever been proven, and there have been no reports of a stranger harming a child with poisoned candy or apples. Anxieties about poisoned candy may have originated from media coverage of  Ronald Clark O'Bryan, a father who gave his own son cyanide-laced candy on Halloween.  [10]
 With the exception of some perishables, most food is edible long after its  expiration date.  [11]  [12]
 Seeds are not the spicy part of  chili peppers. In fact, seeds contain a low amount of  capsaicin, the component which induces the hot sensation in mammals. The highest concentration of capsaicin is located in the  placental tissue to which the seeds are attached.  [13]
  Microwave ovens do not heat food by operating at a special resonance of water molecules in the food. The functional principle of a microwave oven is  dielectric heating rather than resonance frequencies of water, and microwave ovens can therefore operate at many frequencies. Water molecules are exposed to intense electromagnetic fields in strong non-resonant microwaves to create heat. The 22 GHz resonant frequency of isolated water molecules has a wavelength too short to penetrate common foodstuffs to useful depths. The typical oven frequency of  2.45 GHz was chosen partly due to its ability to penetrate a food object of reasonable size, and partly to avoid interference with communication frequencies in use when microwave ovens became commercially available.  [14]
 Microwave ovens do not cook food from the inside out. 2.45 GHz microwaves can only penetrate approximately 1 centimeter (0.39 in) into most foods. The inside portions of thicker foods are mainly heated by heat conducted from the outer portions.  [15]  [16]
 Microwave ovens cannot cause cancer, as  microwave radiation is non-ionizing, and therefore does not have the cancer risks associated with  ionizing radiation such as  X-rays. No studies on the cancer risk associated with microwaves have identified any carcinogenicity from microwave radiation, even with exposure levels far greater than is likely for humans to encounter from leaking ovens.  [17]  [18]
   It is rarely necessary to wait 24 hours before filing a  missing person report. In instances where there is evidence of violence or of an unusual absence, law enforcement agencies in the United States often stress the importance of beginning an investigation promptly.  [19] The UK government website says in large type, "You don't have to wait 24 hours before contacting the police."  [20]
 Twinkies were not claimed to be the cause of San Francisco mayor George Moscone's and supervisor Harvey Milk's murders. In the trial of  Dan White, the defense successfully argued White's  diminished capacity as a result of severe depression. While eating Twinkies was cited as evidence of this depression, it was never claimed to be the cause of the murders. Despite this, people often say that White's attorneys argued that Twinkies made him commit the murders.  [21]
 The  US Armed Forces have generally forbidden military enlistment as a form of  deferred adjudication (that is, an option for convicts to avoid jail time) since the 1980s.  US Navy protocols discourage the practice, while the other four branches have specific regulations against it.  [22]  [23]
 Legal tender laws in the United States do not state that a private business, a person, or an organization must accept cash for payment.  [24]  [25]  [26]
 Sending a document one has produced to oneself in the mail does not give additional copyright protection in the United States  [27] or the United Kingdom.  [28]
 The United States does not require police officers to identify themselves as police in the case of a sting or other undercover work, and police officers may lie when engaged in such work.  [29] Claiming  entrapment as a defense instead focuses on whether the defendant was induced by undue pressure (such as threats) or deception from law enforcement to commit crimes they would not have otherwise committed.  [30]
 Violent  crime in the United States decreased between 1993 and 2017. The violent crime rate fell 49% in that period,  [31] "although most Americans think the number of gun crimes has risen".  [32]
 The  First Amendment to the United States Constitution generally prevents government restrictions on the freedoms of  religion,  speech,  press,  assembly, or  petition,  [33] not restrictions imposed by private individuals or businesses  [34] unless they are  acting on behalf of the government.  [35] Other laws may restrict the ability of private businesses and individuals to restrict the speech of others.  [36]
 Neither the mafia nor other criminal organizations have used  cemented shoes to drown their victims. This method has only been used in single cases to submerge (already) dead bodies.  [37]
     " Edelweiss" is not the  national anthem of  Austria, but the original composition created for the musical   The Sound of Music.  [38] The Austrian national anthem is " Land der Berge, Land am Strome" ("Land of the Mountains, Land on the River").  [39]
 Phil Collins did not sing his 1981 hit " In the Air Tonight" about witnessing someone drowning and then confronting the person in the audience who let it happen.  [40]
 The melody used in " Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star", the " Alphabet Song", and " Baa, Baa, Black Sheep" was not composed by  Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart when he was 5 years old; it was already a popular French  folk tune which existed for decades before Mozart composed  a series of variations on the tune, when he was 25 or 26.  [41]
 Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart was not  Austrian. During Mozart's lifetime,  Salzburg was not part of the  Archduchy of Austria, but an essentially sovereign state called the  Prince-Archbishopric of Salzburg within the  Holy Roman Empire. Salzburg was annexed to the  Austrian Empire in 1805, fourteen years after Mozart's death. Furthermore, the word "Austrian" during the composer's time referred to either the aforementioned Archduchy of Austria, the  Austrian Circle, or the  Habsburg Monarchy, neither of which included Salzburg, which was part of the  Bavarian Circle.  [42]  [43]
 Since the  death of Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart, it has been rumored that the composer was poisoned by his colleague  Antonio Salieri. This has been proven false, as the signs of illness Mozart displayed did not indicate poisoning.  [44] A contributing factor to the survival of this misconception is the highly celebrated  [45] 1984 film   Amadeus, in which Salieri claims he killed Mozart.
 In a very popular YouTube posting, the solo  piano piece " Mariage d'amour" composed by  Paul de Senneville was misattributed to  Frédéric Chopin and was erroneously called "Spring Waltz", despite the fact that it is not a  waltz.  [46]
 The  minuet in G major by  Christian Petzold is commonly attributed to  Johann Sebastian Bach, although the piece was identified in the 1970s as a  movement from a  harpsichord  suite by Petzold. The misconception stems from   Notebook for Anna Magdalena Bach, a book of  sheet music by various composers (mostly Bach) in which the  minuet is found.  [47]  [48]  [49]  [50] Compositions that are doubtful as works of Bach are catalogued as " BWV Anh.", short for " Bach-Werke-Verzeichnis Anhang; Bach works catalogue annex", the minuet being assigned to BWV Anh. 114.
   The  historical Buddha is not known to have been obese. The "chubby Buddha" or "laughing Buddha" is a 10th-century Chinese folk hero by the name of  Budai. In Chinese Buddhist culture, Budai came to be revered as an  incarnation of  Maitreya, the  Bodhisattva who will become a Buddha to restore Buddhism after the teachings of the historical Buddha,  Siddhārtha Gautama, have been forgotten.  [51]
   The  forbidden fruit mentioned in the  Book of Genesis is never identified as an  apple,  [52] a misconception widely depicted in Western art. The original Hebrew texts mention only  tree and  fruit. Early Latin translations use the word  mali, which can mean either "evil" or "apple" depending on if the A is short or long respectively, although the difference in  vowel length had already vanished from speech in Latin at the time. In early Germanic languages the word "apple" and its cognates usually meant simply "fruit". German and French artists commonly depict the fruit as an apple from the 12th century onwards, and  John Milton's   Areopagitica from 1644 explicitly mentions the fruit as an apple.  [53] Jewish scholars have suggested that the fruit could have been a grape, a fig, an apricot, or an  etrog.  [54]
 While they are forbidden by the  Book of Leviticus, having  tattoos does not mean someone  cannot be buried in a Jewish cemetery as is commonly believed, just as violating any other prohibition does not prevent a Jew from ultimately being buried in a Jewish cemetery.  [55]
  The Bible does not say that exactly three  magi came to visit the baby Jesus, nor that they were kings, or rode on camels, or that their names were Casper, Melchior, and Balthazar. The three magi are implied because three gifts are described but we only know that they were plural (at least 2 magi) but could have been many more and probably included an entourage accompanying them on their journey. The  artistic depictions of the nativity have almost always depicted three magi since the 3rd century.  [56] The Bible only specifies an upper limit of 2 years for the interval between the birth and the visit ( Matthew 2:16), and artistic depictions and the closeness of the traditional dates of December 25 and January 6 encourage the popular assumption that the visit took place in the same season as the birth, but later traditions varied, with the visit taken as occurring up to two years later. The association with kings comes from efforts to tie the visit to prophecies in the  Book of Isaiah.  [57]
  The idea that  Mary Magdalene was a prostitute before she met Jesus is not found in the Bible or in any of the other earliest Christian writings. The misconception likely arose due to a conflation between Mary Magdalene,  Mary of Bethany (who  anoints Jesus's feet in  John 11:1–12), and the unnamed "sinful woman" who anoints Jesus's feet in  Luke 7:36–50.  [58]
 Paul the Apostle did not change his name from Saul. He was born a Jew, with Roman citizenship inherited from his father, and thus carried both a Hebrew and a Latin name from birth.  Luke indicates the coexistence of the names in   Acts 13:9: "...Saul, who also is called Paul...".  [59]  [60]
 The term " Immaculate Conception" was not coined to refer to the  virgin birth of Jesus, nor does it reference a belief in the virgin birth of  Mary, his mother. Instead, it denotes a Roman Catholic belief that Mary was not in a state of  original sin from the moment of her own conception.  [61]
 Roman Catholic  dogma does not say that the pope is either  sinless or always infallible.  [62] Catholic dogma since 1870 does state that a dogmatic teaching contained in  divine revelation that is  promulgated by the pope (deliberately, and under certain very specific circumstances; generally called   ex cathedra) is free from error, although official invocation of  papal infallibility  is rare. While most theologians state that  canonizations meet the requisites,  [63] aside from that, most recent popes have finished their reign without a single invocation of infallibility. Otherwise, even when speaking in his official capacity, dogma does not hold that he is free from error.
 St. Peter's Basilica is not the  mother church of Roman Catholicism, nor is it the official seat of the Pope.  [64] These equivalent distinctions belong to the  Archbasilica of Saint John Lateran, which is located in Rome outside of  Vatican City but over which the Vatican has extraterritorial jurisdiction.  [64] This also means that St. Peter's is not a  cathedral in the literal sense of that word.  [64] St. Peter's is, however, used as the principal church for many papal functions.  [64]
 Members of  The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (LDS Church) no longer practice polygamy.  [65]  [66]  [67]  [68] Currently, the LDS Church  excommunicates any members who practice polygamy within the organization.  [69] However, some  Mormon fundamentalist  sects still practice polygamy within their groups.  [70]  [71] For more details on this subject, see  Mormonism and polygamy.
   The  burqa (also spelled burka or burkha) is a female Muslim garment originating in Central Asia that covers the woman's entire body, head and face, including her eyes, with a mesh window or grille for the woman to see. The term is often incorrectly used to refer to other Muslim garments worn by women, such as the  niqab (which shows the eyes) and the  hijab (which shows the entire face but conceals the rest of the body, including the hair).  [72] While the burqa is often light blue or white, the niqab is generally black and the hijab is a  veil that can be of any color.
 A  fatwā is a non-binding legal opinion issued by an  Islamic scholar under  Islamic law; it is therefore commonplace for fatāwā from different authors to disagree. The popular misconception  [73]  [74] that the word means a death sentence probably stems from the fatwā issued by Ayatollah  Ruhollah Khomeini of Iran in 1989 regarding the author  Salman Rushdie, who he stated had earned a death sentence for  blasphemy. This event led to fatāwā gaining widespread media attention in the West.  [75]
 The word " jihad" does not always mean " holy war"; literally, the word in Arabic means "struggle". While there is such a thing as " jihad bil saif", or jihad "by the sword",  [76] many modern Islamic scholars usually say that it implies an effort or struggle of a spiritual kind.  [77]  [78] Scholar  Louay Safi asserts that "misconceptions and misunderstandings regarding the nature of war and peace in Islam are widespread in both the Muslim societies and the West", as much following  9/11 as before.  [79]
 The  Quran does not promise martyrs 72 virgins in heaven. It does mention companions,  houri, to all people—martyr or not—in heaven, but no number is specified. The source for the 72 virgins is a  hadith in  Sunan al-Tirmidhi by  Imam Tirmidhi.  [80]  [81] Hadiths are sayings and acts of the prophet Mohammed as reported by others, and as such they are not part of the Quran itself. Muslims are not meant to necessarily believe all hadiths, and that applies particularly to those hadiths that are weakly sourced, such as this one.  [82] Furthermore, the correct translation of this particular hadith is a matter of debate.  [80] In the same collection of Sunni hadiths, however, the following is judged strong (  hasan sahih): "There are six things with Allah for the  martyr. He is forgiven with the first flow of blood (he suffers), he is shown his place in Paradise, he is protected from punishment in the grave, secured from the greatest terror, the crown of dignity is placed upon his head—and its gems are better than the world and what is in it—he is married to seventy two wives among Al-Huril-'Ayn of Paradise, and he may intercede for seventy of his close relatives."  [83]
   Abner Doubleday did not  invent baseball, nor did it originate in Cooperstown, New York. It is believed to have evolved from other bat-and-ball games such as  cricket and  rounders and first  took its modern form in New York City.  [84]  [85]
 The  black belt in martial arts does not necessarily indicate expert level or mastery. It was introduced for  judo in the 1880s to indicate competency at all of the basic techniques of the sport. Promotion beyond 1st  dan (the first black belt rank) varies among different martial arts. In judo and derived martial arts such as  Brazilian jiu-jitsu, holders of higher master ranks are awarded alternating red and white panels, and the highest grandmasters wear solid red belts.  [86] Other arts such as  taekwondo use black belts with a number of gold bars to indicate the holder's  dan rank.
 The use of  triangular corner flags in English football is not a privilege reserved for those teams that have won an  FA Cup in the past, despite a wide belief to the contrary  [87] that inspired a scene in the film   Twin Town. The  Football Association's rules are silent on the subject, and often the decision over what shape flag to use has been up to the individual club's groundskeepers.  [88]
 India did not withdraw from the  1950 FIFA World Cup because their squad played barefoot, which was against  FIFA regulations.  [89] In reality, India withdrew because the country's managing body, the  All India Football Federation (AIFF), was insufficiently prepared for the team's participation and gave various reasons for withdrawing, including a lack of funding and prioritizing the  Olympics.  [90] However, the myth frequently resurfaces in both India and abroad as fact (especially come World Cup time).  [91]  [92] The AIFF itself may have been the source of this myth.  [90]
    Nonstandard,  slang or  colloquial terms used by English speakers are sometimes alleged not to be real words, despite appearing in numerous dictionaries. All words in English became accepted by being commonly used for a certain period of time; thus, there are many vernacular words currently not accepted as part of the  standard language, or regarded as infelicitous in formal speech or writing, but the idea that they are somehow not words is a misconception.  [93]  [94] Examples of words that are sometimes alleged not to be words include " irregardless",  [95]  [96] "conversate", "funnest",  [97] "mentee", "impactful", and "thusly",  [98] all of which appear in numerous dictionaries as English words.  [99]
 The word " fuck" did not originate in  Christianized Anglo-Saxon England (7th century CE) as an  acronym for " Fornication Under Consent of King"; nor did it originate as an acronym for "For Unlawful Carnal Knowledge", either as a sign posted above  adulterers in the  stocks, or as a  criminal charge against members of the  Brit

title: 常见误解一览表

note:
在我几乎所有的技术职业生涯中，我一直是“远程”工作的直言不讳的支持者--碰巧的是，这份工作是从一份完全偏远的工作开始的。(谢谢您，IBM！)。正如我以前写过的，我一直相信，远程工作是一项从根本上颠覆性的技术，其明显的优势将不可避免地获胜。我认为这件事的真相是显而易见的，不会再长篇大论了。
本周，Facebook宣布允许(一些)员工住在他们想住的任何地方，从而打破了围绕远程薪酬的长期争议。从根本上说，问题是如何“公平”支付远程员工的工资--实际上，这个词到底是什么意思，或者它是否相关。这切中了一些基本问题的核心，这些问题使薪酬成为一个如此艰难的话题。许多公司都在为薪酬而苦苦挣扎，而且大多数都是出于非邪恶的原因。
综上所述，我确实认为有一个正确和错误的答案-有点冷酷的实用主义色彩。
这里有一个务实的部分：如果需要削减工资才能把更多的技术工作岗位从旧金山/纽约市撬到“所有其他地方”，那么就这样吧。即使在理发20%之后，温斯顿-塞勒姆、杰克逊维尔、小石城或布鲁明顿的人们可能仍然会发现Facebook产品营销经理的起薪仍然很有竞争力。我们在工人工资上竞争的雇主越多越好。机会越多越好。太好了。勇敢点儿。
有了这个告诫，让我继续说：生活费调整(COLA)是非常不公平的，也是一种糟糕的做法。做同样工作、提供同样价值的人应该得到同样的报酬。
直到最近，对于大多数人来说，完全远程访问还不是一种选择。如果你想在X公司工作，你必须在某个巨型地铁的办公室工作。这就是我们的交易。
然而，在我工作过的每一家软件公司，几乎每个人的工作都可以--有时也确实是--在网上、远程完成得非常好。是的，全部都有：产品、工程、设计、运营、营销等等。即使是对远程工作持怀疑态度的人也深知这一点。现在，我们正在看到一个现实生活中的概念证明，这一事实每个人都已经知道很长一段时间了。远程团队工作得很好。怀疑者已被确凿地证明是错误的。
换句话说，员工需要在大型地铁工作的商业理由正在崩溃。尽管如此，仍然有许多人可能想要住在纽约-但他们这样做对公司的成功并不关键，甚至不相关。代码并不关心它是在哪里编写的。用户故事、设计样机或卖家支持平台也不需要。如果在坦佩行得通，门洛帕克也会行得通。
(因为这是互联网，人们喜欢吹毛求疵，让我补充一句，有些员工实际上可能有与商业相关的原因住在某个地理区域。特别是，我会考虑直接面向客户的角色，比如销售或客户成功。它们是这条规则的一些罕见的例外。)。
如果基于可乐的薪酬的基本原理是，高成本地铁的员工需要更多的补偿，那么当他们的雇主不再偏爱时会发生什么？另一种说法是：“为什么成本较低地区的员工需要这么多钱”的另一面变成了，公司为什么需要旧金山的员工？
在谈到廉价/昂贵地点的员工时，人们经常会提出这样一个问题，即人们“需要”什么来负担他们的生活方式。这是错误的处理问题的方式，因为它不可避免地充满了个人选择。如果旧金山有人想把收入的一大部分花在他们想住的地方，那是很棒的--也是他们的选择。但是，如果圣马特奥的一名远程(或办公室)员工与他们在小石城的同事做同样的工作，为什么一个人的个人选择会得到奖励，另一个人会受到惩罚？如果小石城的员工有一个孩子，正在照顾年迈的父母，而圣马特奥的员工是单身的，那该怎么办？如果问题是“需要”，为什么这些情况不被认为是相关的呢？简而言之，对可乐进行调整不可避免地会对个人“应该”如何使用他们的钱做出假设，而且大多是不合适的；他们认为生活在大城市是一个值得的选择，而其他决定就不那么值得了。
“如果每个人的工资都是标准的，与低收入社区的同龄人相比，那些生活在高收入地区的人的可自由支配收入会更少。”
根据个人的生活方式选择，可乐不可避免地是对某人的补贴和对另一个人的惩罚。想要住在一个令人向往的、高成本的地铁里，不应该给你的雇主带来商业义务。你住的地方是，而且一直都是一个消费选择。这一选择现在只是形成了更鲜明的对比。
使用COLA最常见的论点是，由于劳动力供求关系，它们是必需的。也就是说：不同地区的市场清算工资不同，因此公司应该根据当地的标准支付工资。这是像GitLab这样的完全分散的公司所采取的基本方法，他们帮助使他们的薪酬公式完全透明。
这种方法的问题来自你对“劳动力供应”的定义。对于一家总部所在地的公司来说，这个劳动力池就像半径20英里内的每个人一样。然而，对于一家偏远的公司来说，它变成了“互联网”。(或者，出于实际目的，您可以在合法司法管辖区雇用的所有网民。(稍后将详细介绍这一点。)。从双方的角度来看，韦恩堡的候选人不仅仅是与当地的候选人“竞争”，她也在与孟菲斯、斯波坎和什里夫波特的候选人竞争。当公司向下调整以达到候选人所在地的市场清理水平时，他们真正谈论的是公司可以惩罚员工并逍遥法外的程度，而不是那个职位的人应该得到什么。在补贴一名员工对另一名员工的地域偏好时，他们陈述的是一种毫无商业意义的组织偏好。
有些人会在这种模式中看到一种“逐底竞赛”。我没有-我认为这是扩大机会。如果其他地方的人能够以较少的薪酬费用完美地完成一项给定的工作，那么让他们做显然是有商业意义的。我可以清楚地理解为什么这会吓到一个在门洛帕克一年赚50万美元的Facebook工程师。考虑到大多数偏远公司所需的稀有人才，无论他们最终付给班戈或里诺接受这份工作的人多少钱，仍然是一笔不错的、高收入的中产阶级收入。
这里有一种滑坡式的相反论点，从逻辑上讲，完全远离意味着好的科技工作将完全从美国消失，并转移到海外。这句话的逻辑结论是，完全远离美国意味着好的科技工作将完全从美国消失，并流向海外。毕竟，海得拉巴、基辅和拉各斯的人们也同样聪明，可以做这些工作中的大多数。从20世纪90年代开始，我们就听到了软件开发行业即将把所有工程角色外包出去的阴暗警告。(说真的，这在90年代末被认为是一个大问题。)。但这不仅没有发生，而且对美国工程师和其他知识型工人的需求也有了相当大的增长。我认为这种情况将继续存在。我认为，主要针对美国观众和公司打造产品，会让美国员工变得有价值。出于远程招聘的目的，与州边界相比，国家边界明显不那么漏洞百出，主要是为了监管和税收问题。
(从政策制定的角度来看，我认为美国领导人为高薪工作的大规模离岸设置监管障碍是有意义的。在没有提供任何替代模式的情况下，关于全球化如何伤害了美国的工人阶级就业，有一些有趣的争论。但这是一篇不同的博客文章，与对该公司的分析水平不同。)。
当谈到薪酬时，很多美国人会说，支付班加罗尔的普加或伊兹密尔的穆斯塔法，就像支付帕洛阿尔托的产品设计师一样，当然是没有意义的。但在这一点上，我感觉到了很多自私自利的想法-普贾和穆斯塔法可能有不同的想法。事实上，如果他们为公司提供相同的价值，我认为正确的做法是向他们支付相同的工资。如果这意味着普贾和穆斯塔法的生活就像国王(/女王)一样，那么就这样吧。对他们来说很好。为离岸团队支付极低的工资相当于基于他们出生的意外而惩罚他们，这甚至比因为他们的生活方式选择而这样做更站不住脚。
这不是会增加补偿成本吗？嗯，我不知道。当然，这会把他们养大的。但以GitLab为例，它已经筹集了大约5亿美元的风投资金。他们有能力支付他们的员工工资。微软或IBM或任何其他在低工资国家拥有数十万员工的大公司的收入低于美国同行的情况又如何呢？我真的不知道那里的答案。正确的是正确的-人们应该得到公平的薪酬，而不是基于国籍。有一种纯粹务实的论点认为，比方说，对班加罗尔来说，许多人的工资低于美国人(但按照当地标准仍然很高)，比起拿到美国工资的少得多的人更好。那不是由我来裁决的问题。但这公平吗？不，它不是。
如果有正当的商业理由要求雇员的所在地，雇主应该向他们支付当地的工资。但如果不是这样的话--对于大多数软件角色来说，情况就是这样--那么劳动力池就会变得比以往任何时候都更广、更深。
随着这种混乱在未来几个月甚至可能几年内逐渐显现，将会有赢家和输家。没有人确切地知道我们将在哪里降落。但是，如果少数几个大型沿海大都市对有利可图的科技公司工作的束缚被打破，我们的社会-和行业-将对此有利。远程工作对公司和团队更有利。这对员工、家庭和社区都更好。并不是每个人都喜欢远程工作，但也不是每个人都喜欢在办公室工作。(我不记得以前有谁的个人喜好很重要。)。
远程工作不会像潮汐一样向我们袭来。在可预见的未来，大多数公司仍将对此犹豫不决。我对COVID危机将如何加速远程工作持乐观态度，但也只是谨慎地乐观。我可能错了，但我认为这将是小步前进，而不是大跃进。但我知道的是：我现在不想持有硅谷写字楼园区的长期商业地产租约。
我会半定期更新我最近读到的最有趣的东西，以及我自己的博客摘要。了解更多信息并阅读老问题，或者直接在下面注册。我不会给你发垃圾邮件的-我保证。

note_en:
I’ve been a vocal proponent of “remote” working for pretty much all of my career in technology – which, as it happens, started off in a fully remote gig. (Thanks, IBM!) My belief all along –  as I’ve written about before – is that remote working is a fundamentally disruptive technology whose clear advantages will inevitably win out. I think the truth in this is pretty obvious and won’t belabor it further.
 This week, Facebook broke open a long-running controversy over remote compensation with its announcement about letting (some) employees live wherever they want. Fundamentally, the question is how to pay remote employees “fairly” – indeed, what that word even means, or if it’s even relevant. This hits right at the core of some basic issues that make compensation such a hard topic. Lots of companies struggle with compensation, and most for non-evil reasons.
 That all said, I do think there’s a right and wrong answer – colored a little bit by cold pragmatism.
  Here’s the pragmatic bit: if it takes cutting wages to pry more tech jobs out of SF/NYC and into “everywhere else,” then so be it. Even after a 20% haircut, folks in Winston-Salem or Jacksonville or Little Rock or Bloomington will probably still find the starting salary of a product marketing manager at Facebook competitive. The more employers we have competing over workers’ wages, the better. Expanded opportunity is  always better. So fine. Go for it.
 With that caveat out of the way, let me go on: cost of living adjustments (COLAs) are deeply unfair and a bad practice. People doing the same jobs and providing the same value should be paid the same.
  Until very recently, going fully remote simply was not an option for most people. If you wanted to work at Company X, you had to be based at an office in some giant metro. That was the deal.
 Yet in every software company I’ve worked for, almost everyone’s job could be – and sometimes was – done perfectly well online, remotely. Yes, all of them: product, engineering, design, ops, marketing and more. Even skeptics of remote work know this, deep down. And right now, we are seeing a real-life proof of concept for this fact that everyone has sorta known for a long time. Remote teams work just fine. The doubters have been conclusively proven wrong.
 In other words, the  business justification for employees needing to be in a major metro is collapsing. There are many who may nevertheless  want to live in, say, NYC – but their doing so is not critical, or indeed even connected, to the company’s success. Code does not care where it’s written. Neither do user stories, design mockups or seller enablement decks. If it works in Tempe, it’ll work in Menlo Park, too.
 (Because this is the internet and people love to nitpitck, let me just add that there are some employees who may  actually have a business-related reason to live in a certain geographic area. In particular, I would think of direct client-facing roles, like sales or customer success. They are some rare exceptions to this rule.)
 If the basic rationale for COLA-based compensation is that employees in high-cost metros need more to be made whole, what happens when their employer no longer has a preference? Said another way: the flip side of “why do employees in cheaper areas need as much money” becomes, rather, why does the company need people in San Francisco?
 This question of what people “need” to afford their lifestyle comes up frequently when talking about employees in cheap/expensive locations. This is the wrong way of approaching the question because it’s fraught with unavoidably personal choices. If someone in San Francisco wants to spend a large chunk of their income on living where they do, then that’s great – and their choice. But if a remote (or office-based) employee in San Mateo does the same work as their colleague in Little Rock, why is one’s personal choice rewarded and the other penalized? What if the Little Rock employee has a child and is caring for an elderly parent, and the San Mateo employee is single and unattached? If the question is “need,” why are those circumstances not deemed relevant? In short, making COLA adjustments inevitably makes assumptions, mostly inappropriate, about what individuals “should” do with their money; and deems living in a big city to be a worthy choice while other decisions to be less so.
  “If everyone is paid a standard salary, those who live in high-income areas would have less discretionary income when compared to their counterparts in lower-income communities.”
  COLAs are unavoidably a subsidy to someone and a penalty to another based on personal lifestyle choices. Wanting to live in a desirable, high-cost metro should not create a business obligation for your employer. Where you live is, and has always been, a consumption choice. That choice is only drawn in sharper contrast now.
  The most common argument for using COLAs is that they’re required due to labor supply and demand. Namely: the market-clearing wage varies across different regions, so companies should pay based on local norms. This is the basic approach taken by such fully distributed companies like Gitlab, who helpfully makes their  compensation formula fully transparent.
 The problem with this approach comes from your definition of the “labor supply.” For an HQ-bound company, that labor pool is something like everyone in a 20-mile radius. For a remote company, however, it becomes “the internet.” (Or, for practical purposes, all internet denizens in legal jurisdictions you can hire in. More on that in a moment.) From both sides’ perspectives, the candidate in Fort Wayne is not “competing” just with those in her local area; she’s competing with the one in Memphis and Spokane and Shreveport, too. When the company adjusts  downward to meet a market-clearing level for the candidate’s location, they’re really talking about how much the company can penalize employees and get away with, not what a person in that role deserves. In subsidizing one employee’s geographic preference over another, they’re stating an organizational preference that makes no business sense.
 Some people will see in this model a “race to the bottom.” I do not – I see it as expanding opportunity. If there are people elsewhere who can do a given job perfectly well for less compensation expense, it obviously makes business sense to let them. I can clearly understand why this would freak out a Facebook engineer making half a million a year in Menlo Park. Given the rarified talent required at most remote companies, whatever they wind up paying the person in Bangor or Reno who takes that job will still be a good, high middle-class income.
  There’s a slippery slope counter-argument here that, taken to its logical conclusion, going fully remote means that good tech jobs will disappear from the U.S. entirely and go overseas. After all, folks in Hyderabad and Kiev and Lagos are just as smart too and could do most of these jobs. Literally since the 1990s, we’ve heard dark warnings that the software development industry was on the verge of offshoring all engineering roles. (Seriously, this was considered a big problem in the late 90s.) But not only has it not happened, but demand for U.S. engineers and other knowledge workers has grown considerably. I think this will continue to be the case. I think there’s something about building products aimed largely at American audiences and companies that makes American employees valuable. For the purposes of remote hiring, national boundaries are meaningfully less porous than state ones, mostly for regulatory and tax issues.
 (From a policymaking perspective, I think it makes sense for U.S. leaders to set up regulatory obstacles to large scale offshoring of high-wage jobs. There are interesting arguments about how globalization has hurt working-class employment in America without offering any alternative model. But that is for a different blog post, and different from the level of analysis of the firm.)
 When it comes to compensation, a lot of Americans would say that  of course it doesn’t make sense to pay Puja in Bangalore or Mustafa in Izmir the same as a product designer in Palo Alto. But in this, I sense a lot of self-interested thinking – Puja and Mustafa probably feel differently. Indeed, if they are providing the same value to the company, I think the right thing to do is to pay them the same wage. If this means that Puja and Mustafa live like kings (/queens), then so be it. Good for them. Paying radically lower wages for offshored teams amounts to penalizing people based on an accident of their birth, which is even less defensible than doing so because of their lifestyle choices.
 Wouldn’t this explode compensation costs? Well, I don’t know. It’ll raise them, sure. But Gitlab, for example, has raised something like half a billion dollars in VC money. They can afford to pay their people. What about Microsoft or IBM or any of the other major companies with hundreds of thousands of employees in low-wage countries making less than their American counterparts? I truly don’t know the answer there. What’s right is right – people should be paid equitably, not on the basis of nationality. There is a purely pragmatic argument that it’s better for, say, Bangalore for lots of people to be paid less than Americans (but still very well by local standards) than for many fewer to be paid U.S. wages. That’s not a question for me to adjudicate. But is it fair? No, it is not.
  Where there is a defensible business justification for requiring an employee’s location, the employer should pay them a local wage. But if not – and for most software roles, this is the case – then the labor pool has just gotten much wider and deeper than ever before.
 There will be winners and losers as this disruption shakes out over the next several months, and probably years. No one quite knows where we’ll land. But to the extent that a handful of giant coastal metro cities’ stranglehold on lucrative tech company jobs is broken, our society – and industry – will be better for it. Remote working is better for companies and teams. It’s better for employees, families and communities. Not everyone loves remote working, but not everyone likes working in an office, either. (I don’t recall anyone’s personal preference ever mattering before.)
 Remote working isn’t going to crash down on us like a tidal wave. For the foreseeable future, most companies will still be hesitant about it. I am optimistic,  but only guardedly so, about how the COVID crisis will accelerate remote working. I could be wrong, but I think this will advance in small steps, not giant leaps. But here’s what I do know: I wouldn’t want to be holding a long-term commercial real estate lease on a Silicon Valley office park right now.

这个模拟游戏的重点是探索通过社会距离和封锁来降低病毒每日传播率的短期努力可能会如何影响感染和死亡。
此仿真基于SIR模型。使用的隔间是敏感的、暴露的、感染的、康复的和康复的。康复车厢进一步分为三个车厢：非住院车厢、住院车厢和超载车厢。产能过剩的死亡率高于住院的死亡率，而非住院的死亡率则不适用。
免责声明：这是一个模拟游戏，并不是试图预测冠状病毒。我们不是流行病学家，这不应该用来预测实际病毒的行为。
在这个国家发现了一种新的病毒。据估计，人们将被感染，导致住院和死亡。疫苗预计在一年内问世。在此之前，你的工作是减缓病毒的传播，挽救尽可能多的生命。你有两种方法来对抗病毒：在整个国家或只在特定的州。小贴士：人们不喜欢别人告诉他们该做什么，如果他们与世隔绝太久，他们可能会违抗命令。
准备好后，单击开始。祝你好运！
人们不喜欢别人告诉他们该做什么。在某一时刻，他们会停止倾听。
note_en:
The point of this simulation game is to explore how infections and deaths might be affected by short term efforts to reduce the daily transmission rates of a virus by social distancing and lockdown.
  This simulation is based on the  SIR model. The compartments used are Susceptible, Exposed, Infected, Recovering, and Recovered. The Recovering compartment is further broken down into three sub-compartments: Non-Hospitalized, Hospitalized, and Over Capacity. Higher death rates are applied to Over Capacity than Hospitalized and no death rates are applied to Non-Hospitalized.
    Disclaimer: This is a simulation game and this is not trying to predict COVID-19. We are not epidemiologists and this should not be used to predict behavior of an actual virus.
  A novel virus has been detected in the country. It is estimated that   people will become infected leading to   hospitalizations and   deaths. A vaccine is expected in one year.   Your job is to slow the spread of the virus and save as many lives as possible until then. You have two ways to fight the virus:  across the entire country or just specific states.   Tip: People don't like to be told what to do and they might disobey if they are isolated for too long.
 When you are ready, click Begin. Best of luck to you!

多年来，关于工作效率的讨论一直在谈论专注、分心、进入工作流等等。随着冠状病毒的流行，不间断的时间变得更加宝贵。我们中的许多人被迫在一个共享的空间里平衡我们的工作和家庭、健康、学校和爱好。我们的生产力和专注度正在受到影响。
一直以来，我们每天都会用到一些可以帮助我们的东西。它的电子邮件！
在你生活中的所有干扰和混乱中，电子邮件是不变的。每个人都有电子邮件，每个电子邮件系统都可以互相发送电子邮件。它可以让我们保持联系，但不需要立即做出回应。你可以在你自己的时间里阅读它，并有空间起草一份深思熟虑的回复。
当我们争先恐后地适应这种新的工作方式时，我们发现视频会议让人精疲力竭。如果没有面对面的交流，不间断的即时通讯聊天会鼓励肤浅的工作，这会让我们感觉效率低下。
在办公室里，你可以看到某人是否看起来在忙于其他事情而不打扰他们--或者如果事情紧急就打断他们。如果没有这种意识，一切都会感到同样紧迫。
任何关注JMAP从最初宣布(2014年)到最终标准化(2019年)的人都知道，电子邮件世界中的事情进展缓慢！这是一件好事。
人们享受着电子邮件的连续性，日复一日地以完全相同的方式工作。电子邮件是稳定和可靠的，你可以直接完成你的工作。在这个瞬息万变的世界里，稳定更加重要。
电子邮件的优势恰恰在于它不是立竿见影的。在你自己的时间里处理你的电子邮件是可以的，而且你不必按照递送的顺序来处理它们。每封电子邮件都有自己的未读状态，因此很容易挑选出要处理的即时消息，然后再返回到其他邮件。
全世界使用电子邮件已有50多年的经验。电子邮件工作流程可以通过强大的概念(如过滤、文件夹和标记或固定)来适应您的需求。
Fastmail是世界上运营时间最长、持续增长的电子邮件公司之一。我们从1995年就开始使用电子邮件了，我们明白永久的重要性，电子邮件是你的电子记忆。我们快速而强大的搜索可以帮助您快速回忆起那些重要的记忆。
我们也明白不受干扰地工作和保持井井有条的重要性，通过Snooze和VIP联系人，您可以控制何时被新电子邮件打扰。
我们将继续让您更轻松地控制您的电子邮件，最近推出了更强大的过滤器，现在还在测试版中添加标签！
如果您还没有使用我们的任何过滤或生产力工具，请不要为此感到压力，但一定要留出时间进行实验。设置一个让您的电子邮件为您工作的工作流程。
您的时间很宝贵。我们在这里建立一个系统，让你可以进入，按照你自己的时间表处理你的电子邮件，然后继续你的余生。许多网站希望你在上面花更多的时间。我们希望您快速处理电子邮件，并有更多时间处理其他优先事项。
不要让你的注意力一次只被一个通知偷走。利用电子邮件的优势，在不被淹没的情况下保持消息灵通。
note_en:
For years, productivity discussions have talked about focus, distractions, getting into the flow, etc., etc. With the coronavirus pandemic, uninterrupted time is even more precious. Many of us are forced to balance our work with home, health, school, and hobbies in one shared space. Our productivity and focus is suffering.
 All along, there’s been something that we use every day, which can help. It's email!
  Through all the interruptions and turmoil in your life, email is a constant. Everyone has email, and every email system can email every other. It lets us stay connected, but doesn’t demand an immediate response. You can read it in your own time, and have the space to craft a thoughtful reply.
 As we scramble to adjust to this new way of working, we're discovering that  video conferences are exhausting. Without in-person communication, the incessant instant messaging chatter encourages  shallow work, which leaves us feeling unproductive.
  In the office, you can see if someone looks busy with something else and not bother them—or interrupt if it’s urgent. Without that awareness, everything feels equally urgent.
  Things move slowly in the email world, as anyone following the progress of JMAP from  initial announcement (2014) to  eventual standardization (2019) knows! This is a good thing.
 People enjoy the continuity of email continuing to work exactly the same way, day after day. Email is stable and dependable, and you can just get your job done. In this rapidly shifting world, stability is even more valuable.
  The advantage of email is precisely that it’s not immediate. It’s ok to deal with your emails on your own time, and you don’t have to process them in order of delivery. Every email has its own unread status, so it’s easy to pick out immediate messages to deal with, and come back to the others later.
 The world has over 50 years of experience with email. Email workflow can adapt to your needs, with powerful concepts like  filtering, folders, and flagging or pinning.
  Fastmail is one the longest-running, and continually growing, email companies in the world. We’ve been working with email since 1995, and we understand the importance of permanence and of email being  your electronic memory. Our  fast and powerful search helps you quickly recall those important memories.
 We also understand the importance of working without interruption and keeping organized, with  snooze and  VIP contacts giving you control over when you’re interrupted by new email.
 We're continuing to make it easier for you to be in control of your email, with  more powerful filters launched recently, and  labels in beta now!
  If you’re not using any of our filtering or productivity tools yet, don’t stress about it, but do set aside time to experiment. Set up a workflow that makes your email work for you.
 Your time is valuable. We’re here building the system lets you get in, deal with your email on your own schedule, and get on with the rest of your life. Many sites want you to spend more time on them. We want you to deal with email quickly and have more time for other priorities.
 Stop your attention being stolen one notification at a time. Take advantage of email to remain well informed without being overwhelmed.

The company will begin allowing certain employees to work remotely full time, he said. Those employees will have to notify the company if they move to a different location by Jan. 1, 2021. As a result, those employees may have their compensations adjusted based on their new locations, Zuckerberg said.
 “We’ll adjust salary to your location at that point,” said Zuckerberg, citing that this is necessary for taxes and accounting. “There’ll be severe ramifications for people who are not honest about this.”
 If there is a market for productivity and accomplishment, the remote worker should be able to get paid the same regardless of location, no? For items where there is a functional market, we can’t say “Oh, this is of excellent quality, but was produced in Cambodia so I am going to pay only half as much as I would pay for the same item, same quality, made in higher-cost China, right?
 Readers: Does the fact that Facebook can unilaterally set the price it will pay for labor depending on the cost of housing from which the labor toils show that the market for Silicon Valley labor is rigged?

海平面--永恒不变。在海洋表面有一个微毫米，在海洋和天空之间移动，两者同时存在，两者都不存在。每一种已知的生命形式都与这一层有关。在它的上方，是土地、空气、阳光和肺部的世界。在它下面，是水、深度和压力的世界。你走得越深，就越黑暗，越充满敌意，越不熟悉，越不慎重，越不为人所知。
去年6月南太平洋的一次水花，标志着那个世界的历史性突破。一台起重机把一个白色的小潜水器从船尾放下来，然后把它扑通一声扔进水里。有一阵子，它在水面上悄悄地上下摆动，它的浮力根据飞行员的重量进行了校准，飞行员是它唯一的乘客。然后他按下开关，潜水艇发出疯狂的、高音的漩涡。电泵将海水吸入一个空房间，将船压得喘不过气来。当水涌入时，水面起泡-然后沉默，因为潜水器的顶部浸入水线以下，海洋吸收了它。
大多数潜艇下潜几百米，然后横跨；这艘潜艇被设计成像石头一样沉没。它的形状像一个鼓起的公文包，底部有一个突出的灯泡。这就是耐压船体-一个直径5英尺的钛球，它与潜水器的其余部分密封在一起，容纳着飞行员和他的所有控制器。乘客座位下面是一个金枪鱼三明治，飞行员的午餐。他凝视着其中一个视口，凝视着蓝色。到达底部需要近四个小时。
阳光穿透了最初的1000英尺深的水。这是上层，浮游生物、海藻和珊瑚礁的一层。它包含了海洋植物的整个生态系统，以及哺乳动物和以它们为食的鱼类。一名埃及潜水员曾下潜到这一层的极限。这一壮举需要终生的训练，四年的计划，一支支援潜水员团队，一系列专门的气罐，以及冗长的13个小时的攀登，不停地减压，这样他的血液才不会中毒，他的肺也不会爆炸。
潜水器以大约每秒2.5英尺的速度下沉。潜水20分钟后，飞行员到达了午夜区域，那里的黑暗水域变成了黑色。唯一的光是微弱的生物发光-来自电凝胶、伪装的虾和带着天然灯笼的有牙齿的捕食者，以吸引不知情的猎物。这些深海的一些鱼没有眼睛--它们有什么用呢？几乎没有什么吃的。午夜地带的条件有利于新陈代谢速度慢、肌肉虚弱、身体粘稠的鱼。
下降一个小时后，飞行员到达了10000英尺--深海地带的起点。温度总是比冰点高几度，不受地表天气的影响。动物以“海洋雪”为食：上层的死鱼和植物的碎片，轻轻地从水柱中飘落下来。延伸到两万英尺的深海带覆盖了97%的海底。
在自由落体两个小时后，飞行员进入了哈达尔区域，该区域以希腊冥界之神的名字命名。它是由海沟(地球构造板块边缘的地质伤痕)组成的，虽然它只占海底的一小部分，但却占到了将近50%的深度。
超过了2.7万英尺，飞行员已经超出了任何一种鱼的理论极限。(它们的细胞在更深的地方坍塌。)。在3.5万英尺后，他开始释放一系列的重量，以减缓他的下降速度。将近7英里的水压在钛球上。如果有任何瑕疵，它可能会立即内爆。
潜艇触碰了淤泥质的海底，53岁的德克萨斯人维克多·韦斯科沃(Victor Vescovo)成为第一个到达汤加海沟最深处的有血有骨的生物。他驾驶着唯一能把人带到那个深度的潜水器：他自己的潜水器。
在接下来的一个小时里，他探索了平淡无奇的米色沉积物，并试图找到并收集岩石样本。然后灯在闪烁，警报器响了。Vescovo检查了他的系统-一号电池发生了灾难性的故障。水已经渗入电子设备，带来了一个不太受欢迎的最好的东西：有史以来最深的人工爆炸发生在距离他头部几英尺的地方。
如果在那个深度有氧气，可能会有一场熊熊大火。取而代之的是，一个电池接线盒熔化了，在外壳上烧了一个洞，没有显示出火焰。由于无法营救，任何恐慌的本能都被压制住了。维斯科沃将不得不自己想办法。
头顶七英里处，一艘白色的船在波利尼西亚水域上下摆动。它曾由美国海军建造，目的是追捕苏联军用潜艇，最近被重新调整用途，以运输和推出韦斯科沃的私人潜艇。船上有几十名机组人员，他们都是Vescovo雇佣的。他试图成为第一个到达每个大洋最深处的人，这一探险被他称为五个迪普斯(Fiep Deep)，当时他已经进行了一半。他在私募股权领域赚了一大笔钱，但他买不到这方面的成功--一个更有钱的人尝试过，但失败了。当这个想法第一次浮现在他脑海中时，他没有可以租用的车辆，甚至没有来自政府的车辆。没有一位科学家或军方有能力进入他试图探访的深度2英里以内。地质学家甚至不确定他应该潜入哪里。
维斯科沃的船员是一个不太可能的集合-正如探险队的首席科学家所说，是“一群真正的小偷”-拥有后勤、工程学、学术界和轻微犯罪的背景。船上的一些人在海上呆了几十年，另一些人是陆上驾驶者。在一年多的时间里，他们面临着像恶劣天气一样永恒的挑战，像他们为这项工作发明的设备一样新颖。他们发现了海底山脉，收集了数千个生物样本，发现了数十个新物种，并烧毁了数万加仑的燃料和酒精。
1969年，当韦斯科沃三岁时，他爬上了母亲的车的前座，这辆车停在他们家门外的小山上。他身材矮小，金发碧眼，是19世纪末来到美国的意大利移民的早熟蓝眼睛孙子，以在南方卖冰激凌为生。维斯科沃把车调到空档。它向后滚到一棵树上，他在重症监护病房度过了接下来的六个星期。有持久的影响：他的右手神经受损，对驾驶复杂车辆的兴趣，以及“折磨人的强迫”，他说，在他死前体验他能体验到的一切。
他从小看科幻小说长大，渴望成为一名宇航员；他有成绩，但没有视力。在斯坦福大学读本科时，他学会了驾驶飞机。之后，他去了麻省理工学院，攻读国防与军备控制研究的硕士学位，在那里他为决策和风险利益建模，这些利益后来汇聚在重叠的职业生涯中，既是一名后备海军情报官员，也是一名商人。韦斯科沃被部署为科索沃爆炸案的目标官员，作为一名反恐官员，他参与了菲律宾的人质营救行动。他学习阿拉伯语，通过金融和咨询工作致富，后来又通过他居住的达拉斯郊区的一家名为Insight Equity的私募股权公司致富。
韦斯科沃开始进行越来越复杂的登山探险，到2014年，他已经滑雪了最后100公里到达北极和南极，并登上了每一大洲的最高峰。他在山顶附近的一次山体滑坡中险些幸免于难。位于阿根廷安第斯山脉的阿空加瓜，并逐渐接受了以计算风险为中心的哲学。控制你能控制的；意识到你不能控制的。在某种程度上，死亡是必然的--“你必须接受它，”他说--他推断，一个人能冒的最大风险就是浪费地球上的时间，在没有最大限度地活着的情况下到达终点。“这是对抗死亡的唯一途径，”他说。“我的社交生活几乎不存在，但这不是优先事项。生活太有趣了。“。他把头发留到肩膀，甚至在胡须变白的时候也染上了颜色。周末，他用自己的私人飞机将救援犬运送到美国各地的潜在主人手中。据他的探险队成员说，在海上，他一个人在船舱里呆了几个小时，玩着使命召唤，吃着微波通心粉和奶酪。
但是，每一个探索的时代都有它自己的路要走。“当沙克尔顿在1914年乘船前往南极时，他仍然可以成为一名英雄。当他在1917年回来时，他不能，“费格斯·弗莱明在欧内斯特·沙克尔顿的日记”南方“的引言中写道。“英雄主义的概念在第一次世界大战的战壕中烟消云散。”当沙克尔顿在南极洲失踪时，他的探险队的一名成员发来电报寻求帮助。温斯顿·丘吉尔回应说：“当所有的伤病员都得到了照顾，当他们所有的贫穷和破碎的家园都得到了重建，当每家医院的钱都充斥着，所有的慈善捐款都被关闭了，那么直到那时。”我很关心这些企鹅。“。
一个世纪后，冒险家们往往会积累更多毫无意义的第一：从山顶上发一条Snapchat。珠穆朗玛峰；在南极洲，用吊杆旅行的最快英里。但没有限制地开放海洋进行探索-韦斯科沃认为，这是一项有意义的记录，可能是地球上最后一项记录。1961年，约翰·F·肯尼迪说：“海洋知识不仅仅是好奇心的问题。我们的生存可能取决于它。“。然而，在接下来的几十年里，距离美国最近的哈达尔海沟成为了制药废物的垃圾场。
2014年9月，Vescovo向佛罗里达州维罗比奇的一家小型制造商Triton Submarines发出了询盘。他指出，他是一名喷气式和直升机飞行员，熟悉“复杂飞行器的程序驱动驾驶”，并概述了后来的五次深度探险。
Triton的总裁帕特里克·莱希在他13岁的时候开始潜水，他发现他在水下的感觉比在陆地上更自在。静默，缓慢而深呼吸的潜水迫使他进入一种冥想状态。“我喜欢失重的感觉，”他告诉我。“我喜欢三维运动，而不是二维运动。”莱希就读于商业潜水学校，学习水坝、桥梁和油气设施的水下焊接和施工。他说：“你可以在水中做的任何事情都可以在水下做。”“你用螺栓固定东西，你切割东西，你把东西焊接在一起，你移动东西，你回收东西。”他补充说，水能导电，有时，“你能感觉到它在牙齿里冒泡。”
1983年，当他21岁的时候，他进行了他的第一次潜艇潜水，下潜到1400英尺的高度，检查北加州海岸的一个石油钻井平台。他被这一经历深深地影响着--前一个小时深入海底，下个小时浮出水面，“没有任何惩罚性的减压，”他说。到维斯科沃联系他时，莱希已经驾驶了60多艘潜水器进行了数千次潜水。一位与他共事数十年的探险队队长告诉我，他“毫无疑问，是世界上最好的潜水艇驾驶员。”
莱希于2007年与人共同创立了Triton。商业模式是为亿万富翁建造私人潜水器，其中包括一名俄罗斯寡头和一名中东王室成员。(在第一个订单之前的几年里，莱希在参加船展时经常被嘲笑；现在有一些公司为游艇建造支援船，用来运载直升机、潜艇和其他昂贵的玩具。)。但他更深层次的愿望是让其他人理解，正如赫尔曼·梅尔维尔在“白鲸”中所写的那样，在河流和海洋中，我们看到了“难以捉摸的生命幻影的形象；而这是一切的关键。”在潜水几次之后，莱希的许多客户开始允许他们的车用于科学和拍摄。
维斯科沃不在乎莱希是否把他用无窗钢球送到海底；他只想到那里去。但莱希拒绝建造任何没有乘客座位的东西，比如科学家；没有用于采集样本的机械手手臂；没有视口的东西，这样乘客就可以享受沉没的感觉。这样的功能会使构建复杂化，甚至可能导致失败。但莱希有一种倾向，在他确定如何实现之前，他倾向于承诺他想要的现实。“这不是一个真正的商业决定，”一位Triton工程师告诉我。“他想要建造这个。放弃不是一种选择。“。莱希将维斯科沃的任务视为开发和测试世界上第一个无限哈达尔探测系统的一种方式-一个可以复制和改进的系统，供科学家使用。
维斯科沃飞往巴哈马，莱希带他乘坐Triton的旗舰潜水器进行了试潜，该潜水器有三个座位，额定深度为3300英尺。第三个座位被一个30多岁的古怪的英国男子约翰·拉姆齐占据，他似乎不喜欢潜水；他全神贯注于他不喜欢的潜水器-这是他设计的。
“我从来没有真正对潜艇有过特别的热情，”拉姆齐告诉我，他是Triton的首席潜艇设计师。“我现在仍然不知道，真的。”他真正喜欢的是，他可以设计每台机器的每一个方面，从中央框架到舱门后部优雅的把手。汽车制造商让整个团队设计座椅或挡泥板，然后规模化生产。但几乎每一艘Triton潜艇都是独一无二的；拉姆齐决定了他想要的样子，佛罗里达州的十几个人开始建造。
拉姆齐在英格兰西南部荒野的一间空余卧室里工作，他从未读过一本关于潜艇的书。“你最终只会完全被你的思维方式玷污，”他说。“我只是想清楚它必须做些什么，然后拿出一个解决方案.”维斯科沃任务的成败在很大程度上掌握在他手中。
“如果维克多死了，这是你的错，你就得自杀，”他告诉他的妻子卡罗琳。
潜水员根据压力、浮力和重量来考虑空间和材料。空气上升，电池下沉；为了实现中性浮力-在水下保持悬浮的能力，而不会上升或下降-每个组件必须与其他组件相互抵消。鱼也是如此，它们通过鱼囊的充气和放气来调节它们的浮力。
拉姆齐的潜艇通常以一个厚厚的丙烯酸球体为中心，本质上是一个气泡；把它释放到水下，它就会弹出水面。但对于维斯科沃的潜水器来说，丙烯酸还不够坚固。在最深的沟渠底部，每平方英寸都要挡住一万六千磅的水--一头站在细高跟鞋上的大象。
拉姆齐固定在钛上：延展性好，耐腐蚀，强度密度比高。耐压船体将重近8000磅。它将不得不由复合泡沫来平衡，复合泡沫是一种由数百万个空心玻璃球组成的浮力填充物。为了让潜艇保持直立，泡沫必须浮在船体上方，提供向上的升力-就像热气球一样，提供水。拉姆齐解释说：“只要重物悬挂在浮力之下保持平衡，潜艇就会一直保持直立。”
船体需要将两块钛板锻造成完美的半球。世界上只有一个设施的舱室足够大、足够强大，可以让船体承受与全海洋深度相当的压力，那就是位于俄罗斯圣彼得堡的克里洛夫国家研究中心(Krylov State Research Center)。莱希参加了压力测试。当时没有后备船体；如果发生内爆，该项目将会终止。“但它奏效了--它证实了我们正在做的事情，”莱希告诉我。
那是2018年仲夏，在南佛罗里达州，Triton的技术人员每天工作15个小时，在一个没有空调的空间里。莱希在车间里走来走去，汗流浃背，试图鼓励他的团队。建造世界上最先进的深潜器的人没有毕业于斯坦福大学或麻省理工学院；他们以前是汽车修理工、潜水教练和水下焊工，因为他们的职业道德和实践经验而被聘用。商店领班过去是一名卡车司机。这位水力学专家在80年代将可卡因运出劳德代尔堡的时候，腹部中了一颗子弹。十几岁时，其中一名电工通过偷汽车收音机来磨练自己的手艺。(“我真的很擅长，”他告诉我。)。拉希则表示，他被联邦政府点名为一起涉及一艘苏联军用潜艇和一个哥伦比亚卡特尔的毒品走私行动中未被起诉的同谋，后来又被宣布无罪。
维斯科沃潜艇的每一个主要部件都必须从头开始研发。石油和天然气行业已经建立了压力额定在6000米左右的零部件供应链-但这只是所需深度的一半。在组装潜艇之前，Triton团队花了几个月的时间在压力室内爆部件，并向制造商发送反馈。拉姆齐说：“你正在解决以前从未存在过的问题，用以前从未存在过的零件，从不知道如何制造它们的供应商那里。”
探险队的其余成员在停靠在维罗海滩港口的一艘船上，等待着。Vescovo留在达拉斯的家中，在Triton在他的车库里组装的模拟器上进行训练。在莱希的推荐下，他聘请了探险队队长兼探险联合创始人罗布·麦卡勒姆(Rob McCallum)，为这个原本可能会梦想破灭的项目注入现实主义。
对于每一个去南极的维斯科沃，都有一个麦卡勒姆确保他活着。(麦卡勒姆去过南极洲128次。)。他对我说：“我喜欢客户走进门来说，‘有人告诉我，这是不可能的，但你怎么看？’”他对我说。“嗯，我认为你刚刚暴露了你的谈判立场。我们喝杯酒好好谈谈吧。“。
麦卡勒姆身材苗条，但胸部丰满，声音柔和，带有新西兰口音。他在巴布亚新几内亚的热带地区长大，后来成为了一名极地导游。他是一名训练有素的医生、潜水大师、消防员、飞机飞行员和船只操作员，曾是新西兰公园护林员，曾担任挪威海军的顾问。他会说三种新美拉尼西亚语言，可以在16英尺深的海浪中为一艘黄道十二宫船驾驶。他是“泰晤士报”“现代爱情”专栏的主角。(“我父亲警告过我要提防你这样的人，”作者回忆道，不管怎样，在嫁给他之前，他曾这样告诉过他。)。麦卡勒姆和他的同事们发现了几艘备受瞩目的沉船，包括澳大利亚和美国的军舰以及一艘以色列军用潜艇。几个月前，他在他的电脑上向我展示了一个声纳扫描的物体，他认为这就是阿米莉亚·埃尔哈特(Amelia Earhart)的飞机。
维斯科沃问麦卡勒姆对他有什么要求。“我首先需要的是你把预算增加两倍，”他回答说。他还驳斥了维斯科沃的几个提议，从过时的(船上没有酒精或配偶)到疯狂的(在船头上安装假军事装备；把他的狗带到地球的最深处)。
五大洋，五深--环游世界，到达两极。麦卡勒姆解释说，探险必须以极地潜水为依托。北冰洋可能的潜水点一年中的大部分时间都被冰覆盖，但有两周的潜水时间。
